sentences.text: I don't know.
I suppose we survived because we were so many in the truck that we warmed each other.
And as we were taken from our home, we took a blanket with us so there was a blanket to lie on and things like that.
But it was absolutely freezing conditions.
You can imagine in December Hungary is not-- and that part of Europe is very cold in December.
It wasn't pleasant.
When we arrived in Belsen, we didn't know what kind of a place this is.
And we were taken to our camp.
And when we went in, we had no German guards at all.
We only had Hungarian guards and mostly our own people who were our guards.
We had a lageralteste.
That's how they called the main leader of the camp.
He was a Jewish man.
And I became very, very friendly with him eventually.
And he helped as much as he could.
And when we got there, the barracks were all two and three-story beds in the barracks.
And I was with a girl whom I knew from Budapest.
And she says, she went-- as she was walking down one of the aisles, she says, come here.
I'm want to show you something.
And she says, look at that inscription on that bed.
And it was an inscription of her sister's.
I was here from that date to that date and taken to Switzerland on such and such a date.
She was taken to Switzerland at the beginning-- the first week of December.
I can't remember the date anymore.
So the story of it is that after the German occupation of Hungary, there were an awful lot of people who paid-- who had money.
And they paid large amounts of money to the Germans because they were promised that they will be exchanged with Switzerland for trucks, and ammunition, and I don't know what.
And the first group went from Budapest a few weeks before us, went to Belsen, and they were exchanged at the, I think the first week of December.
And my friend's sister was in that first group.
When we were taken, by then there were only a handful of people who paid money.
The rest were just like myself and others.
We didn't pay anything.
But we were taken to that camp as well.
And it was called the sonderlager.
And all of the time, we were told that we will be exchanged for trucks and medicines with Switzerland.
But unfortunately, the time hasn't come.
So we were never exchanged.
But we didn't work.
And we wore our own clothes all the time.
We didn't get the striped clothes.
And we had a little bit more food.
We had a piece of bread every day for instance-- what the other camps didn't have.
We had a piece of bread and black coffee in the morning.
And we had a plate of soup at lunchtime.
So that was our food.
I was quite lucky because on the train already, I met a friend of my mother's.
And when we arrived to Belsen, she realized that a relation of her husband's was running the office, because every camp had their own offices.
Our camp had their own office.
So she was working in the office.
And that meant that she had her piece of bread in the office.
And what she got in the camp-- that was mine as well.
So I had two pieces of bread every day.
And so I was a little bit better off than the rest.
She was Jewish.
Oh, yes, they-- we were all Jewish-- every one of us in the camp-- families and--
As I told you, the lagerfuehrer, the boss of the lager, he was Jewish.
And he was my friend.
And I remember that I was smoking in those days, and he had always a few cigarettes.
And when he had cigarettes, then he gave a sign.
And a few of us-- myself and another friend, and some more people-- we went to his bed.
And then he lit up a cigarette.
And the one cigarette went around maybe five of us.
We had all a swiff of the cigarette.
Approximately 1,500 to 1,800.
It was a bit camp, a big camp.
Oh, yes, just for our section, because we were not even allowed to go into the other buildings.
We were not allowed to go.
Hungarians, all Hungarians, all from the-- they weren't bad, you see?
We had nothing-- no nasty things happened to us there, because it was a little bit more sheltered-- our camp from the others.
But I remember that the wire, because every day, we had to go and stand the appel, when we were counted every morning.
And when we were standing outside from the next camp, that was Dutch people in the next camp.
And some could speak German.
And they told us about their life.
So our life was much better than theirs.
Not that it was very good, but it was a bit better.
Yes, yes.
No, only when we saw the notice.
That was our only knowledge about the exchange.
No, no, no, because we didn't have that kind of money.
No.
No, no, no.
We didn't call them capos, no.
Oh, yes, they were all Jewish.
But I mean, they, they didn't do anything bad or anything nasty to us.
They were just the same kind of prisoners as we were.
But let's say in this room now, I say that you are the boss, and we do everything the way you want it.
That's how they were our leaders.
No.
We were-- made friends with each other and talked to each other.
But there were no other social activities.
No, no, nothing.
Actually, at Pesach time, in one of the barracks, I don't-- we didn't know which one, there lived very observant, very religious people.
And they got somehow permission, and they baked matsa, what we eat at Pesach time.
But we didn't see any of it.
It was just in that building, in that camp.
Yes, yes, yes, yes, it did.
It did.
Yes, yes, yes, because of that.
Yes, exist there.
Exist there.
We were existing there.
Yes.
Belsen was liberated on the 15th of April.
And we were put in the wagon about eight or nine days before.
I can't remember the exact date, but the first week of April.
The whole-- yes, our, our camp, yes, because they were still hoping that they can make some money out of us-- some business with us.
And so then we traveled for two weeks again in the open trucks.
But then, somehow, in Belsen we managed to get some wooden planks.
And we put some cover on our open wagons.
There were men with us.
And they managed to, to do it.
And we put some cover half way.
The center of the truck was open.
But the wall at the sides, you know, we had a little shelter from the rain.
Oh, yes, yes, yes.
We were all from there.
We were all from there.
Two weeks.
Yes.
Pretty bad.
Pretty bad.
It was pretty bad.
It wasn't cold by then, but the rain and so on.
And we had a few attack from the train-- the first two or three compartments of the train, there were German soldiers traveling on the sane train, on the same line.
And when the American or British, I don't know who were doing the bombings there, they saw the German soldiers.
And they started shooting our train.
And I remember on one occasion, I had a little cooking pot.
That's what I collected my soup in every day.
And that was-- as I was sitting on the floor in the wagon, the soup pot was just here behind my head.
And when we heard the shooting, some people opened up the wagons and ran down the field.
I didn't.
I just bent down and-- onto the floor.
And when the shooting was over, a bullet went through my pot.
So that's how I'm here.
And a lady, for instance, who was in our wagon with her little boy-- he was about four years old.
She ran down.
And she cuddled her little child like that on the field.
And the child was shot dead.
And the child got a bullet.
Yes, yes, yes.
They didn't know.
They didn't know who we are.
They had no idea, because the first three wagons were full of German soldiers.
Yes.
The little pot which received the bullets--
Received, yes.
For a while, yes.
Yes, I did.
I had nothing to collect my soup in.
Mind you, we didn't get any soup on the train.
In the middle.
In the middle.
Sometimes.
We had no guards.
No guards at all.
I haven't seen any guards in all of the time I was in the camps.
I haven't seen any guards.
No, absolutely nothing-- nothing.
No, only when we got to Theresienstadt.
And we arrived in Theresienstadt.
And we were-- in Theresienstadt there were soldier barracks.
How do you call these buildings-- where the soldiers live always.
And in these buildings were refugees already who were brought there from other places earlier.
And when we arrived, we were taken to one of the squares that was surrounded by these barracks, because they had no idea where to put us at that moment.

And as we were sitting there on the ground on our luggage, tired, and hungry, and everything, my friend who was the boss in our block, he went around the windows and started talking to people that-- where are you from?
What city?
What village?
And you won't believe it, but he found his mother.
She was inside the building.
From their village, she was brought to Theresienstadt.
And she was inside the building.
And they had a little more information about what was going on in the world.
And through these people, we heard that some part of Germany has already been liberated.
And the war is to end very shortly.
But then after that, I got typhoid.
And I was very ill.
So I, I don't know what happened in the next couple of weeks until the liberation.
We were liberated on the 6th of May end of the war.
I don't know.
I don't know-- possibly on the road [INAUDIBLE].
It was in me already.
But I had very, very high temperature.
But every day, my friends pulled me out of bed.
You have to come and stand in the appel, because if they take you to the hospital, then you will never come back.
Yes.
Yes.
So you had to go, despite having high temperature and-- Yes, we had to-- I had to go on the appel every day.
I just dragged myself.
But I had to go, because otherwise, I wouldn't have-- maybe I would have never survived it, because if you go to hospital-- There was no special treatment for somebody who was very sick?
No, no, no.
Food-wise, it wasn't much better.
But at least we were under cover.
And there were reasonable conditions to live in.
And when I was a little bit better, before the liberation, I remember somebody told me that there are people from another Hungarian town where I used to have relations.
There are people from that town.
Come.
We go and visit them.
So we went to visit them.
And there was my late mother's cousin.
And she said to me when she saw me-- my God, she said.
What did you come here for?
As if I could have helped it to come there.
And she was there.
She came from Auschwitz.
She lost her husband.
And she lost a little girl of three by then.
It didn't.
It didn't.
It only affected my mind that I can't eat.
And I have got nothing to eat.
And I would like to eat.
That was all of that was on our mind.
The illness, we didn't even take any notice of it.
I have a high temperature, all right.
It will go.
No, no, only later on.
I presumed.
I don't know.
I'm not a doctor.
But as I had a very high temperature, so everybody said, you must have had it.
There were no German--
--at all in Theresienstadt.
By then, there were not a single German left there.
We couldn't even go out of the building for the first couple of weeks.
Only after the liberation, we could go out, because up till then, the building was locked.
And we couldn't get out of the building to the street.
I don't know if you have ever seen any  pictures or anything Theresien.
But it was a proper little town, Theresienstadt.
And when after the liberation, when we went out, and there was this, the square.
And there was a bandstand in the middle of the square.

And they said that here the musicians, they had to play for the Germans every night and until they were taken to the various death camps, because everybody ended up in the death camps from there-- every one of them.
Yeah, everybody who was moved from there.
Everybody who went to Theresienstadt, that was a sure way of ending up in the death camps.
Just in your case, it was the liberation came.
It was too late.
It was too late.
Yes.
All the Germans had disappeared.
They had fled.
Oh, there was not a single German to be seen.
No, nothing.
And we weren't any better off, unfortunately, even after liberation, because the Russians had nothing.
Oh, yes, the 6th of May-- the 6th of May.
I was better by then.
And we went out for a walk because my friend said, I hear the Russians are coming.
Let's go and meet them.
So we went down to meet them.
And we saw a few horse and cart coming with the Russian soldiers in it.
Certainly, we had-- we couldn't communicate with them or anything.
They haven't had a morsel to give us, nothing.
And our diet was exactly the same after liberation as before, because they had nothing.
I don't know.
I couldn't tell you.
I couldn't answer you that, because we were so lethargic that-- OK, and now what's going to happen now?
The first few weeks we didn't know, because it was just, just the same everything.
Yes, yes.
I quite recovered.
I recovered quite easily afterwards, yes, yes.
Oh, no, no, no no, no, no, nobody, nobody, nobody, nobody.
I just recovered by myself.
No way.
Nobody spoke.
The Russians-- they didn't speak any other language.
And us, nobody spoke Russian.
No, everything was exactly the same as it was before.
Yes, yes, yes.
Ah, yes.
One interesting thing I have to tell you about my homecoming afterwards.
That friend of mine, the gentleman who was our leader, who found his mother-- about three or four weeks after the liberation, he comes to me one day.
He says, I am leaving tomorrow with my mother and another group of people.
We managed to get hold of a-- I think a car or something they got from somewhere.
And we are going home.
Do you want me to take a message to your family at home?
I said, yes, go home and tell wherever you can tell that I am alive, and I survived.
Maybe my mother will look for me.
Ah, that was about beginning of June, let's say.
And I was still in Theresienstadt till about the end of June.
And then when we were eventuality put on the train-- by then it wasn't wagons, we were put on a train-- to go back to Budapest.
And we traveled a good few days.
And I arrived in the main station in Budapest and get off the train.
And who is standing at the platform but my mother.
After the war ended, she went every single day to the Jewish offices where they had, where they got all the news from the various camps.
And through that friend of mine who brought the name of a lot of people, whom he knew who were in Theresien, she got to know that I was alive.
And from that day on, she came every single day to the station to wait for the train to come.
End of June.
The last few days of June, I think arrived about the 30th of June.
Yes, and she was standing there.
Yes, it was a moment.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, yeah.
They have been taken from that ghetto house as well to the same area where my husband's family was under Wallenberg's auspices.
And they survived there-- my mother and my grandmother.
And when that, when the war was over, they went back to our old flat where  we used to live, what was by then given to a non-Jewish family who, who were bombed out from their flat.
An elderly father and his widowed daughter were living in our flat.
So when my mother came back, they were moved into one room.
And they left us the other rooms.
Yes.
